previewLoop: A PhaseAnimator/KeyframeAnimator loops the strike cycle automatically so the end balls swing and alternate sides on their own.
interactiveSpec: Detail uses a DragGesture on an end ball: drag arcs it up about its pivot (angle from translation), and release triggers the eased swing-down that hands off to the auto cradle cycle.

name: Radar Sweep
behavior: A rotating gradient wedge sweeps around a circular grid of range rings, and randomly placed blips flash bright as the beam passes over them, then fade on a phosphor decay.
wow: Blips igniting exactly when the sweep crosses them, then slowly fading like a real CRT phosphor, gives the loader a living, scanning-for-signal narrative.
mechanism: An AngularGradient wedge (transparent→green) rotated by TimelineView angle over concentric Circle range rings. Each blip stores an angle; its brightness = decay of time since the sweep angle last crossed it (phosphor exp falloff). Rendered in Canvas or stacked shapes. iOS 17.
reference: radar/sonar scopes; Find My / scanning UIs
previewLoop: TimelineView rotates the sweep wedge continuously, lighting and decaying blips as the beam passes — fully self-driving.
interactiveSpec: auto — same as previewLoop

name: Sand Hourglass
behavior: Grains stream from the top chamber through a pinched neck and pile into a growing heap below; when the top empties the whole glass flips 180 degrees and the flow reverses.
wow: The falling-grain particle stream plus a heap that builds with an angle-of-repose slope, capped by the satisfying flip, turns a loader into a tiny time-keeping toy.
mechanism: Canvas draws falling grains (each a tiny rect with gravity) plus two Animatable Shapes for the draining top cone and the growing bottom heap (a sloped triangle for angle-of-repose). TimelineView steps the sim; the 180° flip uses rotation3DEffect/rotationEffect with a spring. iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView runs the drain+fall+pile sim on a fixed cycle and auto-triggers the flip when the top empties — self-driving with no tap.
interactiveSpec: Detail adds a TapGesture: tapping flips the glass 180° early (spring), instantly reversing gravity on the grain stream and swapping which chamber drains.
risk: perf: cap grain count and batch in one Canvas; the heap slope is a shape, not per-grain stacking, to keep it cheap

name: Segment Relay
behavior: Twelve discrete rounded segments arranged in a ring light up one-by-one in a baton-pass relay, each lit segment fading on a trailing comet tail as the next ignites.
wow: Discrete segments lighting in sequence reads as crisp and mechanical, the opposite of a smooth conic sweep, and the trailing-fade tail gives it a satisfying rhythm.
mechanism: ForEach of 12 Capsules placed by rotationEffect(.degrees(i*30)) around a ring; TimelineView computes a head index from elapsed time and sets each segment's opacity by its angular distance behind the head for the trailing fade. Not symbolEffect — these are drawn shapes. iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView walks the lit head around the 12 segments continuously, leaving the comet-tail fade — inherently self-driving.
interactiveSpec: auto — same as previewLoop

name: Arc Fan Menu
behavior: A floating button that, on long-press, fans its child actions out along an arc with a staggered spring so each item arrives a beat after the last and overshoots before settling, then sucks them back into the button on release.
wow: The staggered arrival with per-item overshoot makes the menu deal itself out like a hand of cards instead of all items popping at once.
mechanism: Polar layout: each child's offset = (cos/sin of its arc angle) * radius, with radius/opacity driven by an open Bool and a per-item .spring(bounce:0.4).delay(index*0.04) for staggered overshoot. LongPressGesture toggles open; sensoryFeedback on bloom. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView toggles open on a ~2s loop so the items keep dealing out along the arc with staggered overshoot and sucking back into the hub — self-driving, no press.
interactiveSpec: LongPressGesture(minimumDuration:0.2).onEnded toggles open (or .updating to hold-while-pressed); items animate out via the staggered spring on the polar offsets, retract on release. Long-press driven — this is the shipped swiftCode.

name: Collapsing Chevron Crumbs
behavior: A breadcrumb trail where navigating deeper folds older crumbs into a single chevron-stacked accordion chip that springs open on tap to fan out the full path, and re-collapses with a staggered zip when you pick one.
wow: Overflowing crumbs don't get truncated with an ellipsis — they telescope into a folded chevron stack that springs open like a paper fan.
mechanism: matchedGeometryEffect ties each crumb between its stacked (collapsed) and inline (expanded) positions; expanding animates them out with a staggered spring (per-crumb .delay) while collapsed crumbs overlap with small offsets to read as a chevron stack. iOS 17.
reference: Accordion Fold Reveal / Morphing Tab Pill
previewLoop: A TimelineView auto-cycles: deepen (push a crumb into the folded stack), then expand the stack open, then re-collapse — looping the telescope on ~2s beats so the chip keeps folding and fanning.
interactiveSpec: Tap the collapsed chevron chip to toggle expanded; withAnimation(.spring(bounce:0.3)) fans crumbs out via matchedGeometryEffect with index-based stagger, and picking one re-collapses with a staggered zip. Tap-driven.

name: Comet Trail Crumbs
behavior: Each breadcrumb leaves a tapering particle tail toward the next; advancing fires a glowing comet head that flies from the parent crumb to spawn the new child, dragging a dissolving trail.
wow: Navigation depth is drawn as a streaking comet between locations, so the path literally lights up the route you took.
mechanism: Canvas + TimelineView animate the comet head along a path between crumb positions, emitting trail particles each with position/age/opacity that taper and fade; the head uses an additive-style RadialGradient glow. Spawn the child crumb when the comet arrives. iOS 17 (Canvas/TimelineView available 15+).
previewLoop: The TimelineView continuously re-fires the comet hop from one crumb to the next on a loop (~1.5s), respawning the trail each pass so the tile shows a perpetual streaking comet between two crumbs.
interactiveSpec: Tap a crumb (or the advance affordance) to set a target index; the Canvas/TimelineView animates the comet head from current→target position spawning the new crumb on arrival. Tap-driven, particle sim runs in Canvas — this is the shipped wiring.
risk: perf: cap trail particle count (~80) and reuse a fixed buffer; verify Canvas additive glow stays smooth and doesn't accumulate cost on long trails.

name: Concertina Sidebar
behavior: Sidebar rows reveal as an accordion bellows: each row expands from a flattened sliver with a staggered spring so the menu unfolds top-to-bottom like a concertina, and collapses by squeezing back into a stack.
wow: Items don't just fade in — they unfold with bellows-like vertical squash-and-stretch and staggered timing that makes the whole panel feel like it inhales open.
mechanism: Each row applies scaleEffect(y: progress, anchor:.top) + opacity, driven by an open Bool with a per-row staggered .animation(.spring(bounce:0.3).delay(index*0.05)). A faint rotation3DEffect(axis:(1,0,0)) per row adds the bellows pleat catching light. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView toggles the open Bool on a slow breathing loop (~2s open, ~2s closed), so the staggered unfold/refold plays continuously inside the tile.
interactiveSpec: Tap the menu button to toggle open; withAnimation drives each row's staggered scaleEffect(y:)+opacity. Tap-driven, no gesture — this is the shipped wiring.

name: Flipboard Segment
behavior: Each segment label is a row of split-flap tiles; switching segments flips the old label's characters down and the new label's up in a staggered cascade with a tiny mechanical bounce per tile.
wow: The airport split-flap clatter cascades letter-by-letter, turning a mundane control swap into a tactile mechanical reveal.
mechanism: Each character tile is a top/bottom half flipped with rotation3DEffect(.degrees, axis:(1,0,0), anchor:.bottom/.top) driven by a KeyframeAnimator giving each tile a staggered delay and a small overshoot bounce on landing. sensoryFeedback(.impact, weak) per tile click. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A PhaseAnimator auto-swaps between two labels on a timer; each swap re-triggers the staggered per-tile KeyframeAnimator so the board keeps clattering through label changes.
interactiveSpec: Tap a segment to set selectedIndex; the label-change id increments, re-firing each tile's KeyframeAnimator with index-based stagger. Tap-driven, no continuous gesture — this is the shipped wiring.
risk: perf: many per-character KeyframeAnimators with rotation3DEffect; keep label lengths short (<=8 chars) and verify stagger timing on device.

name: Genie Magnify Dock
behavior: A macOS-style dock where icons magnify in a smooth bell-curve as your finger glides across, neighbors gracefully scaling down, and tapping an icon stretches it upward into a genie squash before it pops.
wow: The proper Gaussian magnification falloff plus a genie squash-pop on launch recreates the beloved Mac dock feel natively in SwiftUI.
mechanism: Each icon's scale = base + amplitude * gaussian(distance(iconCenterX, touchX)/sigma), recomputed live so neighbors fall off smoothly; GeometryReader supplies icon frames. The genie launch is a KeyframeAnimator stretching scaleEffect(y:) up then squash-popping. iOS 17.
reference: Reaction Tray Roll (fisheye dock) / macOS Dock
previewLoop: A TimelineView sweeps a synthetic touchX back and forth across the dock on a loop, so the magnification bell-curve travels along the icons continuously — alive without a finger.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture(minimumDistance:0).onChanged feeds location.x into the per-icon gaussian scale; .onEnded (or a tap) on an icon fires the genie KeyframeAnimator squash-pop. Drag-to-magnify is the shipped wiring.

name: Ink Bleed Segment
behavior: A segmented control where tapping a segment drops a spot of ink that organically bleeds outward to fill exactly that segment's bounds with a feathered edge, the previous segment's ink simultaneously draining away.
wow: The fill grows from the tap point like ink soaking into paper rather than a rigid pill snapping into place, so each selection feels hand-stamped.
mechanism: Canvas draws a radial ink blob whose radius is an Animatable progress 0→1, with a feathered edge via a soft RadialGradient fill and slight per-frame jitter on the rim; clip to the segment rect. Drain the old segment with its own progress 1→0. Pure SwiftUI Canvas — no Metal shader. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView drives a looping progress that auto-toggles between two segments (~1.5s each), one blob blooming open as the other drains, so the control keeps re-inking itself.
interactiveSpec: Tap a segment to set selectedIndex and the ink origin point; withAnimation(.easeOut(duration:0.45)) ramps the new segment's bleed progress to 1 and the old to 0. Tap-driven; the bleed origin is the segment center (or tap location if available).

name: Magnetic Grab Drawer
behavior: A thin edge handle that, as your finger nears the screen edge, leans toward and snaps to the touch with a magnetic pull, then stretches a rubbery neck as you drag the drawer out and recoils with a velocity-aware spring on release.
wow: The handle anticipates your finger and lunges to meet it, then the drawer body lags behind on a rubber tether — the panel feels alive and eager.
mechanism: DragGesture drives drawer offset; the handle leans via an attraction curve toward the touch y, and the connecting neck is an Animatable Capsule/Path stretched between handle and drawer body. On release, interpolatingSpring (velocity-aware via predictedEndTranslation) recoils to open/closed. sensoryFeedback(.impact) on the magnetic snap. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView synthesizes a fake finger position cycling toward and away from the edge, replaying handle-lunge → neck-stretch → recoil on a ~2.5s loop so the drawer reaches and snaps back by itself.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture.onChanged sets drawer offset and stretches the neck Path toward the touch; the handle eases toward gesture.location.y via the attraction curve. .onEnded snaps open/closed with .interpolatingSpring seeded by velocity. This drag wiring is the shipped swiftCode.
risk: the rubber-tether neck needs an Animatable Path; verify the stretch follows the handle without kinking and that the magnetic-lean curve doesn't overshoot the edge on device.

name: Magnetic Snap Dock
behavior: A reorderable dock where dragging an icon makes the others slide aside and the dragged icon snaps into slots with a magnetic click and overshoot wobble, while a soft attraction pulls it toward the nearest gap as you hover.
wow: Slots feel magnetized: the icon resists then clicks into place with a springy overshoot, and the gap opens ahead of the drop like the dock is making room for it.
mechanism: DragGesture moves the lifted icon; the live target index is the nearest slot, and the array reorders with matchedGeometryEffect so others shuffle aside via spring. A soft attraction nudges the dragged icon's offset toward the nearest gap center; release snaps with .spring(bounce:0.45) overshoot + sensoryFeedback(.impact). iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView scripts a synthetic drag of one icon back and forth between two slots on a loop, replaying the shuffle-aside and magnetic snap-home so the dock keeps rearranging itself.
interactiveSpec: LongPressGesture.sequenced(before: DragGesture) lifts then moves an icon; onChanged updates target index and shuffles via matchedGeometryEffect; onEnded snaps into the gap with a bouncy spring. This lift-drag-snap wiring is the shipped swiftCode.
risk: matchedGeometry reorder during an active drag can glitch if the source/destination namespace updates mid-gesture; verify the shuffle doesn't fight the drag offset on device.

name: Orbital Satellite Menu
behavior: Tapping the hub launches action icons into a slow orbit ring around it; you rotate the ring with a drag to bring an action to the top selection slot where it enlarges, and a flick spins it with momentum that decays to the nearest item.
wow: The menu is a tiny solar system you can spin with inertia, snapping the chosen satellite into focus like a planetarium dial.
mechanism: Items placed by polar angle around the hub; a DragGesture using atan2(location) updates ring rotation. On release, a TimelineView integrates friction-decayed angular momentum (from gesture velocity) then snaps to the nearest item angle with a spring; the top-slot item scales up. sensoryFeedback ticks as items pass the slot. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView applies a gentle continuous angular velocity to the ring with periodic friction decay and snap, auto-orbiting the satellites and easing one into the focus slot on a loop — no finger.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture.onChanged sets ringAngle via atan2 delta; .onEnded seeds angular velocity from gesture.velocity into a friction-decay TimelineView integrator that coasts and snaps to the nearest item. Rotate/flick driven — this is the shipped swiftCode.
risk: the inertial integrator + snap is fiddly: verify atan2 wrap-around at the ±π seam and that friction decay lands on a detent without jitter on device.

name: Origami Fold Tabs
behavior: The tab bar is a strip of paper panels; tapping a tab folds the bar inward along two crease lines, the chosen icon pops forward off the page with a soft shadow, then the strip unfolds flat.
wow: It reads as physical paper creasing and lifting, with shadow depth selling the fold, instead of any flat highlight sliding sideways.
mechanism: Two HStack panels each driven by rotation3DEffect(angle, axis:(0,1,0), anchor:.leading/.trailing, perspective:0.6) to crease the strip; the selected icon gets a separate forward rotation3DEffect + scaleEffect and a drop shadow whose radius tracks fold progress. Animate with a bouncy spring on the selection state. iOS 17.
reference: iOS Page Cube onboarding / Wallet Card Flip 3D
previewLoop: A PhaseAnimator over the tab indices auto-advances selection on a timer (~1.2s per tab), each switch playing the full crease-in → icon-pop → unfold cycle so the strip looks like it is endlessly folding through its tabs.
interactiveSpec: Tap a tab to set selectedTab; the crease/pop/unfold runs via .animation(.spring(duration:0.5, bounce:0.3), value: selectedTab). No drag needed — tap is the driver; this is the paste-ready swiftCode wiring.
risk: perf: stacked rotation3DEffect layers with shadows can drop frames mid-fold; cap perspective and verify the double-crease anchor math on device.

name: Page Curl Drawer
behavior: Dragging from the screen edge peels the top surface back like a turning page with a curved lit highlight along the lifting edge to reveal a navigation panel underneath, with rubber-band resistance and a spring snap to open/closed on release.
wow: The drawer isn't a sheet that slides over — the current page physically curls away with a lit, shadowed fold edge that tracks your finger.
mechanism: DragGesture from the edge maps translation→curl progress; the top layer uses rotation3DEffect(axis:(0,1,0), anchor:.trailing, perspective) with a LinearGradient highlight+shadow mask along the lifting edge to fake the cylindrical curl. Rubber-band the overscroll, and snap open/closed with .spring on release using predictedEndTranslation. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView ramps curl progress 0→1→0 on a slow loop (~2.5s), auto-peeling the page back to reveal the panel and settling it closed again — self-driving, no finger.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture(minimumDistance:0).onChanged maps location.x to curl progress (with rubber-band beyond bounds); .onEnded snaps to open/closed via .spring keyed on velocity/predictedEndTranslation. This drag wiring is the paste-ready swiftCode.
risk: the gradient-mask curl is an approximation of a true page curl; verify the highlight/shadow tracks the fold edge convincingly on device and that the back layer doesn't z-fight at the 90° crossover.

name: Parallax Deck Tabs
behavior: Tabs live on three stacked depth planes; the selected tab rises toward the viewer while neighbors recede and blur, and a subtle tilt-style parallax shifts the layers as selection moves.
wow: Selection becomes a Z-axis move, not an X-axis slide — the active tab feels like it physically lifts out of a deck of cards.
mechanism: ZStack of tab cards; per-card scaleEffect, blur(radius:), zIndex and a small offset computed from distance-to-selected, plus a light rotation3DEffect for the tilt parallax. Animate all with a single spring on selectedTab. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A TimelineView (or PhaseAnimator over indices) auto-cycles which card is frontmost every ~1s, so the deck continuously lifts one card forward and pushes the rest back with blur — alive without touch.
interactiveSpec: Tap a tab to set selectedTab; .animation(.spring(bounce:0.25), value: selectedTab) drives the Z-rise, neighbor recede and blur. Tap-driven, no gesture wiring — this is the shipped swiftCode.

name: Rolling Bead Tabs
behavior: The active-tab indicator is a solid bead that physically rolls along the tab bar to the selected tab, accelerating and decelerating under friction with a subtle spin, squashing slightly as it brakes and settling under the target icon with a damped wobble; on a long jump it builds real momentum and overshoots before rolling back.
wow: The indicator behaves like a marble with mass and friction rolling across a track, so it has spin, momentum and overshoot rather than sliding rigidly between tabs.
mechanism: The bead's x animates with an overshooting .spring(bounce:0.4) (longer jumps = more overshoot); its rotationEffect spin = travelled distance / circumference, coupled to the same animation. A KeyframeAnimator adds a brake-phase scaleEffect squash (x-stretch then settle wobble). iOS 17.
previewLoop: A PhaseAnimator over the tab indices auto-rolls the bead between tabs on a loop, each move replaying the momentum, spin, overshoot and squash-settle so the marble keeps rolling across the bar.
interactiveSpec: Tap a tab to set selectedIndex; .animation(.spring(bounce:0.4), value: selectedIndex) rolls the bead's x while its rotationEffect tracks distance/circumference and a KeyframeAnimator plays the brake squash. Tap-driven — this is the shipped swiftCode.

name: Ratchet Slide Tabs
behavior: The active-tab indicator advances like a ratcheting mechanism: on switch it steps along a toothed rail one notch at a time with a clicking detent at each tooth, accelerating across longer jumps and braking into a hard stop at the destination tab with a tiny recoil.
wow: The indicator doesn't glide — it ratchets tooth by tooth with detent clicks and a braked hard stop, so reaching a distant tab feels like cranking a mechanical slide into position.
mechanism: A KeyframeAnimator drives the indicator's x through one keyframe per intervening tooth (LinearKeyframe per notch for the stepped feel, accelerating spacing, then a SpringKeyframe brake+recoil at the destination). sensoryFeedback(.impact, weak) fires per tooth crossed. matchedGeometryEffect optionally sizes the pill to the target tab. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A PhaseAnimator over the tab indices auto-steps the selection through the bar on a loop, each switch replaying the tooth-by-tooth ratchet and braked recoil so the indicator perpetually cranks across the rail.
interactiveSpec: Tap a tab to set selectedIndex; the change re-keys the KeyframeAnimator which steps the indicator notch-by-notch to the target with per-tooth haptics and a recoil at the stop. Tap-driven — this is the shipped wiring.

name: Ink Bleed Wipe
behavior: Advancing a page releases a blot of ink at your finger that organically blooms and bleeds outward in irregular wobbling lobes to flood the screen with the next page's color, carrying the new content in behind the wavefront; the idle demo blooms a fresh ink wipe from a corner on a loop.
wow: The transition isn't a hard wipe — it's a living ink blot with breathing edges that spreads like dye in water, themed to each step's color.
mechanism: An animatable blob Shape: a closed Path of radial points whose radius = baseRadius(progress) + sine lobe wobble, filled with the next color and used as a clip mask revealing the incoming page; growth driven by withAnimation on progress 0→1 from the tap location. Pure Shape + Canvas, no shader. iOS 17.
reference: Ink Bleed Toggle / Ink-Fill Radio; dye-in-water transitions
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator (or TimelineView) blooms progress 0→1 from a corner anchor with the lobe wobble animating, holds, then resets to the next theme color — a self-running ink wipe.
interactiveSpec: Tap (SpatialTapGesture) gives the bloom origin; on tap, withAnimation(.easeOut) drives blob progress 0→1 from that point, the wobbling-lobe Shape clip-masking in the next page/color behind the wavefront.
risk: irregular lobe wobble must stay smooth (animate radius params, not re-seeded noise per frame); verify clip-mask perf as the blob fills the full screen

name: Constellation Hero
behavior: Drifting star points float in the hero area and on each page animate drawing connecting lines between specific stars to trace a themed constellation (heart, bell, key) which then twinkles; swiping re-routes the lines to form the next page's figure, and it auto-redraws on a loop.
wow: The connect-the-dots line-drawing reveal turns each onboarding concept into a little night-sky myth that literally wires itself together before your eyes.
mechanism: Stars drift via TimelineView(.animation) sine offsets; the constellation edges are a Path stroked with an animatable trim(from:0,to:progress) (Shape with animatableData) so the line draws point-to-point; star twinkle uses opacity .easeInOut on a TimelineView. iOS 17.
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator drives trim 0→1 (draw) → twinkle → trim back → next figure on a loop; stars keep drifting via TimelineView, so the constellation self-wires and re-routes with no finger.
interactiveSpec: Horizontal DragGesture/TabView swipe: page progress maps to trim 'to' for the outgoing figure (un-draws) and the incoming figure (draws) so lines re-route as you scrub; .onEnded snaps progress to 0 or 1.

name: Depth Diorama Swipe
behavior: Each page is a 4-layer code-drawn scene (sky, hills, midground props, foreground) that scrolls at different speeds as you drag so the world has real depth; released between pages it springs to settle with the layers overshooting at parallaxed rates, and the auto-loop pans the camera side to side.
wow: A believable diorama of depth built entirely from SwiftUI shapes and gradients — no image assets — where each layer lags by its distance like a real pop-up book.
mechanism: TabView(.page) or a horizontal ScrollView whose page progress drives per-layer .offset(x: progress * depthFactor) (sky ~0.1 → foreground ~1.0); layers are gradient-filled Shapes, and scrollTransition / visualEffect applies the depth multiplier per page. spring(response:) gives the settle overshoot. iOS 17.
reference: Headspace onboarding parallax; weather app layered scenes
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) pans a camera x on a slow sine ±; each layer offsets by progress × its depthFactor so the parallax pans gently side to side on a loop without touch.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture translation.width / pageWidth → page progress; each layer reads that via visualEffect to offset by depthFactor; .onEnded uses predictedEndTranslation to snap to the next/prev page with an interpolatingSpring so layers overshoot at their own rates.

name: Ferrofluid Dots
behavior: Page dots behave like magnetized ferrofluid: as your drag passes between them the two nearest dots stretch toward each other and merge into a gooey metaball bridge, then the trailing dot releases and snaps back round; the auto-loop sweeps an invisible magnet across the row.
wow: Real gooey metaball merging done purely with Canvas + alphaThreshold/blur filters — the dots feel like living mercury reacting to your finger.
mechanism: Canvas draws the dots into a layer wrapped in addFilter(.blur(radius:)) then addFilter(.alphaThreshold(min:)) so overlapping blurred circles fuse into a single metaball blob; dot center positions are interpolated toward the drag x, all in Canvas with no Metal shader needed. iOS 17 (Canvas filters since 15).
reference: Dynamic Island gooey merge; classic ferrofluid metaballs
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) sweeps a virtual magnet x across the row on a sine over ~2s; the two nearest dot centers pull together, fuse through the alphaThreshold blob, then release — fully self-driving.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture.location feeds the magnet x; the two nearest dots' centers ease toward it (distance falloff) so the Canvas metaball bridge forms under the finger; .onEnded springs centers back to their rail slots.
risk: metaball needs Canvas .blur + .alphaThreshold tuned together (radius vs threshold); verify fusion/separation thresholds on device, watch redraw cost

name: Odometer Steps
behavior: A horizontal step counter where the current step is a split-flap tile that flips '01' to '02' digit-by-digit on each advance while the surrounding dots slide along a rail and recolor; idle, it auto-flips through the sequence and loops.
wow: Turns a boring '3 of 5' into a tactile mechanical split-flap board, complete with the satisfying staggered card-drop cascade.
mechanism: Split-flap tile = top/bottom halves each rotation3DEffect(.degrees, axis:.x, anchor:.bottom/.top) with perspective, the falling top half driven by a spring so it overshoots into the seam; surrounding dots use matchedGeometryEffect to slide on the rail and contentTransition(.numericText) only for the recolor/value tick. iOS 17.
reference: Solari split-flap departures board; iOS flip-clock widgets
previewLoop: A PhaseAnimator advances the step value 1→2→3→1 on a timer; each advance triggers the two-half flip with a staggered .delay per digit so the card-drop cascade plays itself.
interactiveSpec: Horizontal DragGesture (or TabView page swipe): translation.width past a half-tile threshold commits the next step .onEnded, firing the rotation3DEffect flip sequence + matchedGeometry dot slide; below threshold springs back.

name: Origami Hero Unfold
behavior: The hero starts as a folded paper triangle that unfolds crease-by-crease through several 3D-hinged flaps into a full flat figure with subtle paper-fold shadows; swiping refolds it and unfolds the next shape, and idle it gently folds and unfolds on a breathing loop.
wow: Sequential creased unfolding with gradient shadows on each panel sells real folded paper — a tactile origami reveal you rarely see done in pure SwiftUI.
mechanism: Each flap is a triangular Shape with rotation3DEffect along its crease edge (axis set per panel, anchor on the shared crease) plus a LinearGradient overlay whose opacity tracks the fold angle for fold-shadow shading; panels unfold in sequence via PhaseAnimator with staggered springs. iOS 17.
reference: Origami / paper-fold onboarding heroes (Pinch Origami Fold sibling)
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator cycles fold angles 1→0→1 (folded↔unfolded) on a gentle timer; per-panel .delay makes creases open in order and the gradient shadows shift, all self-driving.
interactiveSpec: Horizontal DragGesture/TabView swipe: page progress maps to the master fold parameter (1=folded triangle → 0=flat figure) driving every flap's crease angle live; .onEnded snaps folded/unfolded by threshold, swapping to the next figure when committed.
risk: rotation3DEffect crease anchors must share exact edge coords per panel or flaps visibly separate; verify fold ordering/z-index and shadow direction on device

name: Pop-Up Book
behavior: Turning to a page hinges flat shape-drawn scenery upward off the spine using 3D rotation so the illustration physically pops up from the fold as the previous one folds back down; the auto-demo turns pages on a timer with the classic stand-up-then-lay-flat motion.
wow: Recreates the unmistakable mechanics of a children's pop-up book — scenery rotating up on hidden hinges along the center spine — entirely in SwiftUI 3D transforms.
mechanism: Scenery pieces are gradient Shapes with rotation3DEffect(.degrees(0→90), axis:.x, anchor:.bottom) and a projection with perspective (m34) to hinge up from the spine line; incoming page rotates 90→0 while outgoing rotates 0→-90, sequenced via PhaseAnimator with a spring for the stand-up snap. iOS 17.
reference: Children's pop-up books; Page Cube perspective onboarding
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator cycles a page index on a timer; the .x-axis hinge angles tween 90→0 (rise) for the new layer and 0→-90 (lay flat) for the old, so the pop-up stand-up/lay-down plays on a loop.
interactiveSpec: Horizontal DragGesture/TabView swipe: translation.width → 0–1 turn progress mapped to the hinge angles (incoming rises, outgoing folds) live; .onEnded commits or springs back based on threshold + predicted velocity.
risk: rotation3DEffect anchor + perspective must align to a shared spine line or pieces detach visually; verify hinge anchors and z-ordering during the cross-fold

name: Page Tear Reveal
behavior: Dragging from the page edge tears the current page away along a jagged ripped-paper edge that follows your finger, revealing the next page underneath with a soft torn-fiber shadow; release past threshold completes the tear with a flick, snaps back otherwise, and the auto-loop tears a page every few seconds.
wow: A procedurally jagged tear line drawn live with Canvas that ripples under your finger — it genuinely feels like ripping a sheet off a notepad.
mechanism: The torn edge is an animatable Shape: a Path of small randomized zig-zag segments (seeded so it's stable) whose split position = drag progress, used both as the top page's clip mask and as a slightly offset darker fill for the torn-fiber shadow; Canvas can render the ragged fibers. Underlying page sits beneath. spring on release. iOS 17.
reference: Tear-Off Receipt / coupon tear; ripped-notepad reveal
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator ramps tear progress 0→1 (page peels off along the jagged line) → reset → repeat on a ~2.5s loop, with a tiny sine wobble on the edge so it ripples — no finger.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture from the edge: translation → tear progress positioning the jagged split + clip mask live; .onEnded checks progress/predictedEnd vs threshold — past it flicks the torn piece off with a spring + falls away, else springs the seam closed.
risk: seed the jagged Path so the edge stays stable per frame (don't re-randomize every redraw); verify clip-mask + shadow alignment, cap Canvas redraw

name: Wake-the-Bell Permission
behavior: A sleeping notification-bell hero sits dim until you long-press 'Allow', which wakes it: the bell rocks harder and harder, sound-wave rings pulse out, a red badge pops with a spring, and on release it settles with a happy nod; the auto-loop performs the full wake-and-ring cycle.
wow: Turns the dreaded notifications prompt into a charming 'wake up the bell' toy where holding the button literally winds it up until it rings.
mechanism: Bell is an SF Symbol / Shape with rotationEffect(anchor:.top) oscillating at an amplitude tied to hold progress; sound-wave rings are Circles with trim+scale+opacity on a TimelineView; the badge pops via .spring(bounce:0.6) scale and symbolEffect(.bounce); sensoryFeedback on lock. iOS 17 (uses rotationEffect rock, not the iOS 18 .wiggle symbolEffect).
reference: iOS notification-permission prompt reimagined; Apple Pay-style reward
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator runs sleep → winding rock (amplitude ramps) → ring pulses + badge pop → settle nod on a loop; the rocking and rings auto-drive so it rings itself with no finger.
interactiveSpec: LongPressGesture (or DragGesture(minimumDistance:0) for live progress): hold duration ramps the rock amplitude and ring rate; release past the wind-up threshold fires the badge spring + sensoryFeedback(.success), else the bell calms back to sleep.

name: Radar Lock-On Permission
behavior: A location-permission hero shows a sweeping radar that, when you grant access, catches a wandering blip, draws a reticle that snaps inward to lock onto it, drops a pin that bounces in, and ripples a 'located' ring; idle, the sweep keeps hunting and periodically performs the lock-on for the demo loop.
wow: The reticle-snap-then-pin-drop choreography makes granting location feel like a satisfying GPS lock-on rather than a scary privacy ask.
mechanism: TimelineView(.animation) drives the sweep arm angle and a fading trailing gradient wedge (Canvas or rotated AngularGradient); the wandering blip moves on a sine; granting runs a KeyframeAnimator with independent tracks for reticle scale-in (snap), pin .spring(bounce) drop, and an expanding 'located' ring (Circle scale+opacity); sensoryFeedback on lock. iOS 17.
reference: Find My / GPS lock-on; Apple Pay success ring choreography
previewLoop: TimelineView keeps the sweep + blip hunting continuously; a PhaseAnimator periodically fires the lock-on KeyframeAnimator (reticle snap → pin bounce → ring ripple) then resets to hunting — fully self-driving.
interactiveSpec: Tap on 'Allow' (TapGesture): on tap, run the KeyframeAnimator lock-on sequence — reticle scales in onto the blip's current position, pin springs down, located ring ripples out, with sensoryFeedback(.success); sweep pauses then resumes hunting.
risk: perf: drive sweep/trail via one TimelineView+Canvas rather than repeatForever stacks; keep the trailing wedge gradient cheap

name: Symbol Assembly Hero
behavior: The page's hero icon assembles from a scattered cloud of smaller dots and strokes that fly in and lock together into a crisp SF Symbol, then settles with a bounce; swiping to the next page explodes it back into particles that re-converge into the new symbol, and it auto-cycles when idle.
wow: Every hero feels hand-built in front of you as dozens of fragments swarm into place with staggered springs — a self-constructing logo moment for each step.
mechanism: Canvas (or ForEach of small Circles) animates each particle from a random scattered position to its target offset sampled around the symbol, with staggered .spring(bounce:0.5).delay(per-index); on lock the real SF Symbol Image crossfades in and plays symbolEffect(.bounce) for the settle. iOS 17.
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator cycles assemble → hold → explode → reassemble (next symbol) on a ~2s timer; particles lerp scattered↔target with per-index delay so the swarm self-constructs and re-forms without touch.
interactiveSpec: TabView/horizontal DragGesture swipe: crossing the page threshold .onEnded triggers explode-out (particles → scattered) then converge-in to the next symbol; in-flight translation can drive partial scatter for scrubbing.
risk: perf: cap particle count (~40–60) and prefer one Canvas over many animated views to keep the swarm at 60fps

name: Worm Page Dots
behavior: As you swipe between pages the active indicator stretches into a connecting pill that elongates toward the destination dot then snaps closed around it, like an inchworm crawling along the track; left idle it auto-crawls forward through the pages on a timer.
wow: The indicator never teleports — it physically reaches across the gap and reels itself in with a spring, so your eye always tracks where it's going.
mechanism: An HStack of Capsules where the active pill is a single Capsule whose leading/trailing x are driven independently off a 0–1 page-progress value (lead edge moves first, trail follows) via withAnimation(.spring(bounce:0.4)); progress comes from the TabView selection or a DragGesture translation/width. iOS 17.
previewLoop: A PhaseAnimator cycling page index 0→1→2→0 drives the same lead/trail progress so the pill auto-crawls dot to dot on a ~1.2s loop with spring snap-close — no finger.
interactiveSpec: TabView(selection:) page style OR a DragGesture on the dot row: translation.width / rowWidth → page progress; lead edge tracks finger, trail edge lags via spring, snaps to nearest dot on .onEnded by predicted end.

name: Caustic Glass Lens
behavior: A draggable circular glass blob magnifies and refracts the content beneath it, with animated underwater caustic light bands rippling across the lens interior. Idle, it drifts in a slow figure-eight as a self-running demo.
wow: It feels like sliding a real water droplet across the screen — edges bend, light pools, and caustics swim inside, not a flat tap-ripple.
mechanism: .layerEffect (iOS 17) is the right tool: it both samples displaced coords to magnify/refract the backdrop AND composites the caustic light band in one pass (distortionEffect alone can't add light). Shader uniforms are the lens center + radius + time; time comes from TimelineView(.animation) and center from a DragGesture location. Min iOS 17.
reference: iOS 26 Liquid Glass / a magnifying water droplet
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) drives the lens center along a parametric figure-eight (sin t, sin 2t) while time keeps the caustics rippling — auto-cycles with no finger so the droplet drifts forever.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture(minimumDistance: 0) feeds .location into the shader's center uniform so the lens tracks the finger; on .onEnded it springs back to the figure-eight idle. TimelineView(.animation) still supplies time for the live caustics.
risk: specific — refraction reads neighbors far outside the view: set a generous maxSampleOffset and guard edge sampling, then verify magnification + caustics on device.

name: Chromatic Velocity
behavior: RGB channels of an image split apart proportional to drag or scroll velocity, smearing into a prismatic streak on fast flicks and snapping back to crisp on release. An auto loop oscillates the split with an eased sine.
wow: Speed becomes color — the faster you fling, the wider the prism splits, giving motion a tangible chromatic exhaust trail.
mechanism: .layerEffect (iOS 17) — must be layerEffect, not colorEffect, because it samples the layer three times at offset coords (one per R/G/B) and recombines. The split-distance uniform is fed by gesture velocity; an interpolatingSpring eases it back to 0 on release. Min iOS 17.
reference: Arc Search / glitchy motion-blur RGB shift
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) drives the split uniform with an eased sin wave (0→max→0) so the prism opens and closes on its own as a non-interactive tile demo.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture maps live translation delta (or .velocity) per frame to the split uniform — fast drags widen the prism; .onEnded resets the uniform to 0 with .spring so it snaps crisp.
risk: low — three layer.sample() calls per pixel; cap split magnitude so offsets stay within a modest maxSampleOffset.

name: CRT Power-On
behavior: Content renders through a vintage CRT with scanlines, rolling sync bars, phosphor bloom and barrel vignette. Tapping triggers the power-on flash that collapses to a horizontal line then blooms outward, and it idles with a slow scanline roll.
wow: The nostalgic 'thunk' of an old TV switching on — the picture snaps to a glowing line and blooms back, scanlines breathing the whole time.
mechanism: .colorEffect (iOS 17) is sufficient: per-pixel it adds scanline/vignette/phosphor color and gates visibility with a luminance mask band (the collapse-to-a-line is a mask, not real geometry warp). Uniforms are time + a powerOn progress value; pair with sensoryFeedback for the 'thunk'. Min iOS 17.
reference: Apple II / VLC CRT filter / old TV power-on
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) advances time so scanlines and the rolling sync bar drift continuously, and a PhaseAnimator periodically re-fires the power-on collapse→bloom so the tile keeps switching on by itself.
interactiveSpec: auto — same as previewLoop, but the collapse→bloom is fired by .onTapGesture animating the powerOn uniform 1→0→1, with sensoryFeedback(.impact) on the bloom. (For a true geometry squish, layer a scaleEffect; the colorEffect mask alone reads as the classic line.)
risk: low — per-pixel color only, fully self-contained.

name: Develop
behavior: Content starts as huge mosaic blocks and resolves to full resolution as you drag a slider-thumb across it, like a developing Polaroid revealing pixel by pixel. The auto loop dissolves from blocks to sharp and back on a timer.
wow: Pixelation becomes a tactile reveal — sliding your thumb 'develops' the image from chunky blocks into crisp detail in real time.
mechanism: .layerEffect (iOS 17) snaps each pixel's sample coordinate to the center of a grid cell, then animates the cell size from large→1px. The cell-size uniform is driven by a normalized slider progress value (0→1). Min iOS 17.
previewLoop: PhaseAnimator or TimelineView(.animation) ramps the cell-size uniform big→1px→big on a slow eased timer, so the tile auto-develops and re-pixelates without input.
interactiveSpec: A DragGesture along a track maps thumb x (clamped 0…1) directly to the cell-size uniform so dragging right develops the image; release holds the current position.
risk: low — single grid-snap sample per pixel, self-contained.

name: Foil Tilt
behavior: Holographic foil-stamp diffraction: dragging tilts the virtual viewing angle so iridescent rainbow sheen sweeps across a sticker's embossed pattern with a sharp specular glint. Idle, it auto-rocks the tilt to keep the foil shimmering.
wow: It nails the exact rainbow shimmer of a foil trading-card sticker catching the light — a thin diffraction glint races across as you tilt, no flat gradient.
mechanism: .colorEffect (iOS 17) computes per-pixel iridescence as a function of pixel position and a tilt uniform (hue from a diffraction-angle term, plus a narrow specular glint band). Tilt is fed by a DragGesture vector; no neighbor sampling needed. Min iOS 17.
reference: Holographic trading card / Wallet card sheen
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) auto-rocks the tilt uniform with a slow sine on x and y so the rainbow sheen and glint sweep back and forth on their own.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture maps .translation (or device-relative drag vector) to the tilt uniform so the user tilts the virtual viewing angle; .onEnded springs tilt back to the auto-rock idle.
risk: low — per-pixel color math only, fully self-contained.

name: Halftone Press
behavior: The image is reduced to a rotating-grid duotone halftone of dots whose radius tracks underlying luminance. Pinch scales the dot frequency from coarse newsprint to fine, and the auto loop slowly orbits the screen angle and dot size.
wow: Pinching dials a real risograph dot-screen in and out — coarse comic-book dots smoothly resolve into a continuous-tone print before your eyes.
mechanism: .layerEffect (iOS 17) samples each rotated grid cell's luminance to set that cell's dot radius, then renders the dot as duotone. Uniforms are dot frequency + screen angle; frequency is fed by MagnifyGesture scale. Min iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) slowly orbits the screen-angle uniform and eases dot frequency coarse↔fine so the tile auto-cycles between comic-book dots and continuous tone.
interactiveSpec: MagnifyGesture maps .magnification to the dot-frequency uniform (pinch out = finer, pinch in = coarser), clamped to a sane range; release holds the current frequency.
risk: low — per-cell luminance read; clamp frequency so cell sizes stay reasonable on device.

name: Heat Mirage
behavior: Pixels bend with stacked noise octaves to create rising heat-shimmer, with an intensity hotspot you drag to 'aim the heat'. Left alone, it ripples a wandering thermal column up the view.
wow: The air above the content visibly boils and wobbles like asphalt in summer, and you can physically point where the heat rises.
mechanism: .distortionEffect (iOS 17) returns a sample coordinate offset by summed noise octaves that scroll upward over time; uniforms are time + hotspot point + intensity. Time is driven by TimelineView(.animation); hotspot by a DragGesture location. Min iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) advances the noise time so the shimmer column rises continuously, and the hotspot slowly wanders (lerp between random points) — fully self-driving with no finger.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture(minimumDistance: 0) feeds .location into the hotspot uniform so the user 'aims' where heat rises; intensity ramps near the touch and decays on release. TimelineView(.animation) keeps supplying time.
risk: low — pure per-pixel coordinate warp; keep maxSampleOffset above peak shimmer amplitude to avoid edge clipping.

name: Jelly Wobble
behavior: Flinging the view sends a springy shockwave that jiggles the pixels like a block of jelly with directional inertia and decaying overshoot. The demo loop nudges it with periodic random impulses so it never sits still.
wow: The entire surface has gelatin physics — flick it and the whole panel ripples, sloshes, and settles with satisfying springy overshoot.
mechanism: .distortionEffect (iOS 17) offsets sample coords by a radial damped-sine shockwave emanating from an impulse origin with directional bias. Uniforms are impulse origin + amplitude + time; amplitude is seeded by DragGesture velocity and decays via the in-shader damping. Min iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) re-seeds the impulse with a random origin and velocity at periodic intervals, so the panel keeps getting nudged and sloshing on its own.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture(minimumDistance: 0) on .onEnded reads predictedEndTranslation/velocity to set the shockwave amplitude and direction from the release point; the shader's damped sine settles it over time.
risk: low — radial coordinate warp; cap amplitude under maxSampleOffset so the wobble doesn't clip at edges.

name: Mercury Pour
behavior: A metaball threshold makes the content's bright areas behave like liquid metal beads that merge and split with a chrome specular rim as you drag a gravity direction. The auto loop tilts gravity in a slow circle, pouring the mercury around.
wow: Highlights gather into gleaming quicksilver droplets that gloop together and pull apart with surface tension as you tilt where the metal flows.
mechanism: .layerEffect (iOS 17) over a pre-blurred copy of the content: sample the blurred layer, apply an alpha/luminance threshold to form metaball beads, then add a chrome specular rim from the gradient. Uniforms are gravity direction + time; gravity is fed by a DragGesture vector. Min iOS 17.
reference: Dynamic Island gooey merge / liquid metal
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) sweeps the gravity-direction uniform around a slow circle so the beads pour and recombine continuously, no finger.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture maps the translation vector to the gravity-direction uniform so dragging tilts where the mercury flows; release eases gravity back to the auto circular sweep.
risk: HIGH — metaball needs a blurred input plus many samples per pixel; cap blur radius and bead count, and verify performance + the threshold edge on device.

name: Wax Melt
behavior: A long-press heats the view so the pixels sag and drip downward into molten wax strands that pool at the bottom, then re-solidify upward when released. An auto loop melts and reforms on a slow cycle.
wow: The UI literally melts under your finger like a hot candle, dripping in viscous strands, then crawls back into shape — uncomfortably physical.
mechanism: .distortionEffect (iOS 17) pulls each pixel's sample coord downward by a per-column noise-weighted amount scaled by a melt uniform (0→1), forming drip strands. Min iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView(.animation) drives the melt uniform up and back down on a slow eased sine (solid→molten→solid) so the tile drips and reforms on a loop, no finger.
interactiveSpec: Use a press-state driver — .onLongPressGesture(pressing:) or DragGesture(minimumDistance: 0) — so the melt uniform ramps up via .animation while held and reverses on release; a one-shot LongPressGesture can't ramp.
risk: specific — drip length can exceed the view; set maxSampleOffset to cover full strand length or the wax clips, and verify on device.

name: Conic Prism
behavior: A full-bleed conic gradient with hard discrete color stops (no soft blend) spins slowly like a color wheel while a second counter-rotating banded conic layered with a blend mode creates shifting moire pinwheel spokes.
wow: The crisp banded spokes and counter-spin moire give a sharp graphic, almost vinyl-record identity that is deliberately the opposite of soft aurora gradients — and it is a handful of lines of pure SwiftUI.
mechanism: Two AngularGradient layers with hard stops (duplicated stops at equal locations for crisp bands); one rotationEffect spins +θ, the other −θ, composited with .blendMode(.difference/.screen) for moire spokes. Drive both angles from a single TimelineView(.animation) or PhaseAnimator. Pure gradient + rotation, iOS 17.
previewLoop: auto — same as previewLoop: TimelineView slowly counter-rotates the two banded conics so the moire pinwheel spokes drift continuously inside the tile.
interactiveSpec: auto — same as previewLoop.
risk: low — two gradients and two rotations, trivial to render.

name: Constellation Mesh
behavior: Free-floating points drift with gentle velocity in a Canvas and draw a line between any two points within a proximity radius (with opacity falling off by distance), forming a constantly reforming network graph; a tap drops a new bright node that the web instantly latches onto.
wow: The self-assembling, breathing web of connections feels like a living neural net, and tapping to seed a node that the lattice rushes to connect is quietly addictive.
mechanism: Canvas + TimelineView(.animation): each frame integrates node positions by velocity (bounce off bounds), then draws a line stroke between every pair within radius with opacity = 1 − dist/radius. Tap appends a bright node to the node array. Pure Canvas, iOS 17.
previewLoop: The nodes already drift via TimelineView so the web perpetually reforms with no finger; a synthetic node injected and retired on a timeline cadence (every ~2s at a cycled location) makes the lattice rush to latch on, then it ages out — self-driving.
interactiveSpec: SpatialTapGesture (iOS 16) gives the tap location; append a bright, slightly larger node there with a small random velocity. The per-frame proximity pass instantly draws links from it to nearby nodes; optionally fade its glow over a few seconds.
risk: perf: link detection is O(n²) — cap node count (~40–60) so the per-frame pairwise pass stays cheap.

name: Contour Topo
behavior: Stacked horizontal contour lines are generated from a sum of slowly phase-shifting sine waves and rendered as flowing topographic ridgelines with subtle banded fills between them, so the whole map looks like a relief surface gently morphing and scrolling like wind over dunes.
wow: It evokes an animated topographic map or oscilloscope terrain — structured, line-driven and architectural rather than blobby — and the layered ridgelines create real depth from pure 2D paths.
mechanism: Canvas builds N stacked Paths; each ridgeline's y = baseline + Σ sin(x·freqᵢ + phaseᵢ + time) with per-line offset, filled with a faint band between adjacent ridges and stroked on top. TimelineView(.animation) advances the phases so the relief morphs and scrolls. Pure Canvas Paths, iOS 17.
previewLoop: auto — same as previewLoop: TimelineView phase-shifts the summed sines so the topographic ridgelines flow and scroll like wind over dunes inside the tile.
interactiveSpec: auto — same as previewLoop.
risk: low — a few dozen sampled Paths; keep sample step coarse and ridge count modest to stay smooth.

name: Depth Starfield
behavior: Three parallax layers of point-stars (far/mid/near, each with its own size, brightness and twinkle) scroll opposite to a drag with depth-weighted speed so near stars streak past and far stars barely move; with no input the field auto-drifts forward as a slow warp toward a vanishing point.
wow: Genuine multi-layer depth parallax from pure point math — flicking it sends near stars streaking like a hyperspace nudge, and the idle slow-warp reads as effortless cinematic space.
mechanism: Canvas draws three precomputed star arrays (far/mid/near) each with its own parallax multiplier, size and twinkle phase. TimelineView(.animation) advances a forward-warp time so stars stream outward from a vanishing point; twinkle = a sine on per-star phase. DragGesture translation offsets each layer by its depth weight. iOS 17, no shaders.
reference: Classic Starfield / hyperspace screensaver (seed: Starfield Parallax — Canvas + TimelineView).
previewLoop: TimelineView auto-drifts the warp toward the vanishing point so stars continuously stream forward and twinkle with no touch — the cinematic idle state is the loop.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture translation shifts each layer by its depth multiplier (near streaks, far barely moves); on end, feed predictedEndTranslation into an interpolatingSpring so a flick sends near stars streaking past like a hyperspace nudge before easing back to the forward drift.
risk: low — point math only; cap stars per layer (~150) to keep Canvas cheap.

name: Hex Pulse
behavior: A tight honeycomb of hexagon cells pulses scale and brightness in expanding rings radiating from the center on a timer, so a glowing wave continually washes outward across the tessellation and fades at the edges.
wow: The radial breathing wave across a perfect honeycomb gives a clean sci-fi HUD energy, distinct from any rotation-based tile and from soft gradient fields.
mechanism: A hexagon Shape laid out in an offset honeycomb grid (ForEach with per-row x-offset); each cell's scale + brightness = a sine of (time − distanceFromCenter × k), so a phase-delayed wave radiates outward. TimelineView(.animation) (or PhaseAnimator) advances time. Pure Shape grid, iOS 17.
previewLoop: auto — same as previewLoop: TimelineView advances the radial phase so a glowing pulse continually washes outward across the honeycomb and fades at the tile edges.
interactiveSpec: auto — same as previewLoop.
risk: low — a static hex grid with per-cell scale/opacity from a cheap sine; cap rows/cols for the tile.

name: Magnetic Dot Field
behavior: A precise lattice of small dots drawn in a single Canvas where each dot is pushed away from and scaled by its distance to the touch point like iron filings repelled by a magnet, with springy return; when untouched an invisible attractor auto-orbits the screen so the field ripples on its own.
wow: True per-dot magnetism (displacement + scale + spring), not a water-distortion blur — it feels tactile and electric, and the idle auto-orbit makes a grid tile look alive without any touch.
mechanism: A single Canvas draws the dot lattice; each dot's offset and radius are a falloff function of its distance to an active CGPoint. TimelineView(.animation) interpolates dots toward their rest position with a spring-style lerp each frame. DragGesture updates the active point; on end it eases back. All pure Canvas math, iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView moves the attractor point on a slow Lissajous/orbit path around the tile so a magnetic ripple wanders the lattice continuously with no finger — the same displacement+scale math the drag drives.
interactiveSpec: DragGesture sets the live attractor to value.location each frame; every dot repels and scales by distance falloff. On gesture end, animate the field back to the resting lattice with an interpolatingSpring so the dots settle with a slight overshoot.
risk: perf: per-frame distance calc over the full lattice — cap dot count (~300–500) and keep the falloff cheap; otherwise low.

name: Truchet Flow
behavior: A grid of Truchet tiles (each a pair of quarter-circle arcs) continuously rotates in 90-degree steps along a diagonal wave so the connecting lines reroute into endlessly shifting maze-like loops and circuits across the whole surface.
wow: Watching the connected curves perpetually re-thread themselves into new labyrinths is hypnotic, and it is built from one tiny rotating Shape repeated in a grid.
mechanism: One quarter-arc-pair Shape repeated across a LazyVGrid/ForEach grid; each tile's rotationEffect is a quantized 90° step whose phase = f(row+col diagonal index + time). TimelineView(.animation) (or a single PhaseAnimator cycling the diagonal phase) advances the wave so tiles flip in a traveling diagonal. Pure Shape + rotation, iOS 17.
previewLoop: auto — same as previewLoop: the TimelineView/PhaseAnimator phase advances the diagonal flip wave forever, so the connecting curves re-thread into new labyrinths on their own inside the tile.
interactiveSpec: auto — same as previewLoop.
risk: low — small grid of one cached Shape with cheap rotation.

name: Voronoi Fracture
behavior: A Canvas renders a coarse field of ~24 Voronoi cells with crisp seams; the cell seeds slowly orbit so the whole stained-glass tessellation creeps and re-tiles while each cell cycles hue, and a tap fractures the nearest cell into a brief splinter-burst before it heals back into the mosaic.
wow: It reads like living stained glass — hard-edged and crystalline instead of soft aurora drift — and the tap-to-shatter gives a satisfying physical crack that knits itself back together.
mechanism: TimelineView(.animation) drives a Canvas that, per render, advances ~24 orbiting seed points and fills each pixel region with its nearest seed's hue (compute cell polygons on a coarse grid, not per-pixel, to stay CPU-cheap). Tap fracture spawns short-lived splinter Paths from the hit cell, animated via an explosionProgress state on a spring. Pure Canvas + GraphicsContext, iOS 17.
previewLoop: TimelineView already auto-orbits the seeds so the mosaic perpetually creeps and re-hues with no finger; a synthetic tap location cycled around the tile by the timeline (e.g. date-driven CGPoint every ~2.5s) auto-fires a fracture-and-heal so the tile shatters on its own.
interactiveSpec: SpatialTapGesture (iOS 16) reports the tap location; map it to the nearest seed, set that cell's explosionProgress to 1 and animate back to 0 with a snappy spring so the tapped cell splinters then re-knits. The continuous seed orbit keeps running underneath.
risk: perf: nearest-of-N-seeds fill is O(samples×seeds) on CPU — render on a coarse sample grid (or downscale the Canvas) and cap seeds ~24; verify frame rate on device.
